---
title: "Research"
open-graph:
  type: article
  url: https://ecomperat.github.io/research.html
  article:
    author: Étienne Compérat
    section: Research
format:
  html:
    toc: false
---

My research studies how institutions, infrastructure, and markets shaped long-run development, with a primary focus on nineteenth-century France. I am particularly interested in spatially uneven modernisation: why the same shocks that integrated rural areas into national markets, schools, language, and politics generated convergence in some places but concentration, migration, and deindustrialisation in others. Empirically, I construct original historical datasets from archival, textual, administrative, and geospatial sources, combining quantitative methods with GIS, OCR, computer vision, and reproducible workflows.

## Doctoral Project

<div class="research-paper">

<p class="research-paper-title">Diffusion and Divergence: Railways, Schools, and the Modernisation of Rural France</p>

<div class="research-badges">
<span class="research-badge">Economic History</span>
<span class="research-badge">Political Economy</span>
<span class="research-badge">Spatial &amp; Regional Economics</span>
</div>

<div class="research-abstract">
<span class="research-abstract-label">Abstract</span>
<span class="research-abstract-body">This project asks how railway-induced market integration and mass primary education reshaped the geography of development in nineteenth-century rural France. The dissertation treats railways and schooling as modernisation shocks that integrated rural communes into national markets, institutions, and language, while also reallocating people, firms, capital, and activity across space. The central question is why these shocks generated integration and convergence in some places but concentration, out-migration, and deindustrialisation in others.</span>
</div>

<ul class="sample-work-list">
<li>
<span class="swl-title">Railways, Schools, and the Making of France</span>
<span class="swl-desc">Studies whether railway expansion produced linguistic integration and political mobilisation, and how railway access interacted with primary schooling.</span>
</li>
<li>
<span class="swl-title">Education and the Restructuring of Rural Industry</span>
<span class="swl-desc">Extends my master's thesis by asking whether the Guizot Law's schooling shock accumulated human capital locally or reallocated it toward industrialising centres.</span>
</li>
<li>
<span class="swl-title">Railways, Market Access, and Competition</span>
<span class="swl-desc">Connects the market-access and trade-shock literatures to study when railway integration generated expansion, restructuring, or decline in agriculture and industry.</span>
</li>
</ul>

</div>

## Master's Thesis

<div class="research-paper">

<p class="research-paper-title">"Enrichissez-vous!": Education, Suffrage, and Local Development in July Monarchy France</p>

<div class="research-badges">
<span class="research-badge">Master's Thesis · Sciences Po · 2025</span>
<span class="research-badge research-badge-award">Sciences Po Prize for Best Master's Thesis (2024-2025)</span>
</div>

<p class="research-advisors">Jury: <a href="https://sites.google.com/view/galbiatir1/">Roberto Galbiati</a>, <a href="https://sites.google.com/site/emericmlhenry/home">Emeric Henry</a> (supervisors), <a href="https://kevinhorourke.com/">Kevin O'Rourke</a></p>

<div class="research-abstract">
<span class="research-abstract-label">Abstract</span>
<span class="research-abstract-body">This paper studies how primary schooling and municipal suffrage shaped local development in July Monarchy France. I combine the 1831 Municipal Law and the 1833 Guizot Law with an arrondissement-level dataset covering 1830-1865. Pre-reform male primary education is positively associated with industrial wages in the 1840s, but I find no robust independent or interactive effect of municipal suffrage and no statistically significant medium-run wage effect of policy-induced schooling expansion. The paper also shows that threshold-based designs in this setting face historically informative identification challenges because commune-size distributions are spatially structured and correlated with prior development.</span>
</div>

<div class="research-links">
<a class="research-link" href="pdf/COMPERAT.Etienne_masters_thesis.pdf">PDF</a>
</div>

</div>

## Predoctoral Research

As a predoctoral fellow at UZH under Hans-Joachim Voth, I contribute to the following projects.

<div class="predoc-item">
<span class="swl-title">(Untitled)</span>
<span class="swl-meta">with <strong>M. P. Squicciarini and N. Voigtländer</strong></span>
<div class="predoc-desc">I help build reproducible historical data pipelines from archival, textual, administrative, and geospatial sources for a project set in Revolutionary France, including historical geocoding, GIS integration, and background work on period historiography.</div>
</div>

<div class="predoc-item">
<a class="swl-title" href="https://www.jvoth.com/images_july_24.pdf">Image(s)</a>
<span class="swl-meta">with <strong>D. Yanagizawa-Drott</strong></span>
<div class="predoc-desc">I contribute to the computer-vision workflow, including image preprocessing, labelling and validation, supervised model training, feature extraction, name extraction, and appendix analysis of name norm-deviation.</div>
</div>

<div class="predoc-item">
<a class="swl-title" href="https://brunocaprettini.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/napoleon.pdf">Fighting for Growth</a>
<span class="swl-meta">with <strong>B. Caprettini and A. Trew</strong></span>
<div class="predoc-desc">I contributed to the documented replication package by writing clean, reproducible code for the empirical analysis.</div>
</div>

For details on additional research assistance and replication experience, feel free to consult my [Resume](cv.html).

## Selected Sample Work

A selection of assignments from my graduate coursework. Browse the full [Sample Work](coursework.html) page for more.

<ul class="sample-work-list">

<li>
<span class="swl-title">Railroads, Market Access, and Urban Fringe Dynamics: Extensions to Donaldson &amp; Hornbeck (2016)</span>
<span class="swl-meta"><strong>Research Proposal</strong> · Urban &amp; Regional Economics | Sciences Po · 2025</span>
<span class="swl-desc">This proposal, a precursor to the market-access component of my doctoral project, outlines extensions to the Donaldson-Hornbeck framework: one incorporating dynamic spatial adjustment and urban fringe land-use equilibria, and one adapting the approach to nineteenth-century France using railway maps, agricultural inquiries, and historical census data. [<a href="pdf/URE_researchproject.pdf">PDF</a>]</span>
</li>

<li>
<span class="swl-title">Primary Schooling and Crime in 19th-Century France: A Cohort-Based Identification Strategy</span>
<span class="swl-meta"><strong>Research Proposal</strong> · Topics in Political, Public &amp; Organizational Economics | Sciences Po · 2025</span>
<span class="swl-desc">Proposes a causal identification strategy for the effect of the 1833 Guizot Law on adult crime using cohort exposure as an instrument for literacy, estimated in a panel fixed-effects framework with nineteenth-century administrative data. [<a href="pdf/TPPOE_researchproject.pdf">PDF</a>]</span>
</li>

<li>
<span class="swl-title">Replication of Monge-Naranjo, Sanchez &amp; Santaeulalia-Llopis (2019)</span>
<span class="swl-meta"><strong>Replication Package</strong> · Computational Economics | Sciences Po · 2024 · with <a href="https://lionelchambon.github.io/">Lionel Chambon</a> and <a href="https://www.paulogcd.com/">Paulo Gugelmo Cavalheiro Dias</a></span>
<span class="swl-desc">Full Julia replication package for "Natural Resources and Global Misallocation" (AEJ: Macroeconomics), reconstructed from scratch. The full replication package, documentation, and reproducible workflow are available <a href="https://www.paulogcd.com/Replication_Monge_et_al_2019.jl/dev/">here</a>.</span>
</li>

</ul>
